items.correct: Yes — plants respire all the time to release energy; they also photosynthesise when there is light.
items.misconception: Plants only photosynthesise and do not respire; respiration is for animals only.
items.random_wrong: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day; the processes never overlap.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is taught as the plant equivalent of respiration, students think it replaces respiration in plants entirely.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: What is produced in anaerobic respiration in human muscle cells?
items.correct: Lactic acid (lactate) and a small amount of ATP — no CO2 or water in this pathway.
items.misconception: Anaerobic respiration produces ethanol and CO2 in humans.
items.random_wrong: Anaerobic respiration produces oxygen as a waste product.
items.why: Students confuse anaerobic respiration in yeast (alcohol + CO2) with the human form (lactic acid).

items.q: Do plants only respire at night?
items.correct: No — plants respire all the time, day and night. In daylight they also photosynthesise, often faster than they respire.
items.misconception: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day.
items.random_wrong: Plants never respire because photosynthesis gives them all their energy.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is obvious in daylight, students assume it replaces respiration, but every living cell respires continuously to release energy.

items.q: Is energy 'created' as it passes along a food chain?
items.correct: No — energy is transferred and much is lost as heat at each step; it is never created.
items.misconception: Energy is created at each link in a food chain so predators have the most.
items.random_wrong: Energy is destroyed completely at the first trophic level.
items.why: Students think top predators are 'most powerful' so must gain energy, but energy only flows and dissipates — it cannot be created, obeying conservation of energy.

items.q: Where in the cell does aerobic respiration happen?
items.why: It's Mitochondrion. “Chloroplast” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does yeast produce by anaerobic respiration?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ethanol + carbon dioxide. “Lactic acid only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A leaf cell respires 30 glucose molecules aerobically. Each glucose needs 6 oxygen molecules. How many oxygen molecules are used in total?
items.why: The real figure is 180. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
